content:
1. If $A, B, C$ are the angles of a $\triangle A B C$, then

$$ \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \sin 2 A & \sin C & \sin B \\ \sin C & \sin 2 B & \sin A \\ \sin B & \sin A & \sin 2 C \end{array}\right| \text { is equal to } $$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { } \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \sin A \cos A & \sin C & \sin B \\ \sin C & 2 \sin B \cos B & \sin A \\ \sin B & \sin A & 2 \sin C \cos C \end{array}\right| \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 k a \cos A & k c & k b \\ k c & 2 k b \cos B & k a \\ k b & k a & 2 k c \cos C \end{array}\right| \\ & {\left[\because \frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b}=\frac{\sin C}{c}=k\right]} \end{aligned} $$

$$ =k^3\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 a \cos A & c & b \\ c & 2 b \cos B & a \\ b & a & 2 c \cos c \end{array}\right| $$

$$ =k^3 \left\lvert\, \begin{array}{ll} a \cos A+a \cos A & a \cos B+b \cos A \\ a \cos B+b \cos A & b \cos B+b \cos B \\ c \cos A+a \cos C & b \cos C+c \cos B \end{array}\right. $$

$$ \left.\begin{aligned} & c \cos A+a \cos C \\ & b \cos C+c \cos B \\ & c \cos C+c \cos C \end{aligned} \right\rvert\, $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left.=k^3\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \cos A & a & 0 \\ \cos B & b & 0 \\ \cos C & c & 0 \end{array}\right|| | \begin{array}{ccc} a & \cos A & 0 \\ b & \cos B & 0 \\ c & \cos C & 0 \end{array} \right\rvert\, \\ & =k^3 \times 0=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $ A=\frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ a & 2 & b\end{array}\right] $ is an orthogonal matrix, then
explanation:
We know that, if $ A $ is an orthogonal matrix, then

$ A A^{T}=I $

$ \begin{array}{c} \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ a & 2 & b \end{array}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & a \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 & b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & -2 \\ a & 2 & b \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & a \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 & b \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 9 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 9 \end{array}\right] \\ \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 9 & 0 & a+4+2 b \\ 0 & 9 & 2 a+2-2 b \\ a+2 b & 2 a+2-2 b & a^{2}+4+b^{2} \end{array}\right] \\ =\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 9 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 9 \end{array}\right] \end{array} $

$ \Rightarrow \quad a+4+2 b=0 \text { and } 2 a+2-2 b=0 $

$ \Rightarrow \quad a+2 b=-4 \quad \ldots \text { (i) } $

and $ a-b=-1 \quad \ldots \text { (ii) } $

By solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$ a=-2, b=-1 $

content: Suppose $ p, q, r \neq 0 $ and system of equation $ (p+a) x+b y+c z=0 $, $ a x+(q+b) y+c z=0 $, $ a x+b y+(r+c) z=0 $, has a non-trivial solution, then the value of $ \frac{a}{p}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{c}{r} $ is
explanation:
Let $ |A|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}p+a & b & c \\ a & q+b & c \\ a & b & r+c\end{array}\right| $

Also, given that equations has non trivial solution $ \therefore $

$ |A|=0 $

$ \begin{aligned} & \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} p+a & b & c \\ a & q+b & c \\ a & b & r+c \end{array}\right|=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (p+a)[(q+b)(r+c)-b c]-b[a(r+c)-c a] +c[a b-a(q+b)]=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (p+a)[q r+q c+b r]-b[a r]+c[-a q]=0 \\ \Rightarrow & p q r+p q c+p b r+a q r=0 \end{aligned} $

Dividing whole equation by $ p q r $

$ \begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{p q r}{p q r}+\frac{p q c}{p q r}+\frac{p r b}{p q r}+\frac{q r a}{p q r}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 1+\frac{c}{r}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{a}{p}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{a}{p}+\frac{b}{q}+\frac{c}{r}=-1 \end{array} $

Given the matrix $ A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $ and $ A^{-1} = \frac{1}{k} \operatorname{adj}(A) $, we want to determine the value of $ k $.

We know from the properties of matrices that:

$ A^{-1} = \frac{\operatorname{adj}(A)}{|A|} $

By comparing this equation with the given relationship $ A^{-1} = \frac{1}{k} \operatorname{adj}(A) $, we can identify that:

$ k = |A| $

Therefore, we need to calculate the determinant $|A|$ of the matrix $ A $:

$ |A| = \left| \begin{array}{ccc} 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{array} \right| $

To find $|A|$, expand across the first row:

$ |A| = 3 \begin{vmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} - (-2) \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} + 4 \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} $

Calculate each of the 2x2 determinants:

$ \begin{vmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (2 \times 1) - (-1 \times 1) = 2 + 1 = 3 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (1 \times 1) - (0 \times -1) = 1 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (1 \times 1) - (0 \times 2) = 1 $

Substitute these values back into the determinant calculation:

$ |A| = 3 \times 3 + 2 \times 1 + 4 \times 1 = 9 + 2 + 4 = 15 $

Thus, the value of $ k $ is $|A| = 15$.

content: If the system of linear equation $$3 x-2 y+z=2, 4 x-3 y+3 z=-5$$ and $$7 x-5 y+\lambda z=9$$ has no solution, then $$\lambda$$ equals to
explanation:
To determine the value of $$\lambda$$ for which the given system of linear equations has no solution, we need to analyze the consistency of these equations. The given system is:

$$3x - 2y + z = 2$$ (Equation 1)

$$4x - 3y + 3z = -5$$ (Equation 2)

$$7x - 5y + \lambda z = 9$$ (Equation 3)

For a system of linear equations to have no solution, the equations must be inconsistent, meaning they do not intersect at any point. This happens when the determinant of the coefficient matrix is zero, and the augmented matrix creates a contradiction.

First, let's form the coefficient matrix and calculate its determinant. The coefficient matrix $$A$$ of the system is:

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 4 & -3 & 3 \\ 7 & -5 & \lambda \end{pmatrix}$$

The determinant of matrix $$A$$ is given by:

$$\text{det}(A) = \begin{vmatrix} 3 & -2 & 1 \\ 4 & -3 & 3 \\ 7 & -5 & \lambda \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding this determinant along the first row, we get:

$$\text{det}(A) = 3\begin{vmatrix} -3 & 3 \\ -5 & \lambda \end{vmatrix} - (-2)\begin{vmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 7 & \lambda \end{vmatrix} + 1\begin{vmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ 7 & -5 \end{vmatrix}$$

Now, calculate these 2x2 determinants:

$$\begin{vmatrix} -3 & 3 \\ -5 & \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (-3)(\lambda) - (3)(-5) = -3\lambda + 15$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 7 & \lambda \end{vmatrix} = (4)(\lambda) - (3)(7) = 4\lambda - 21$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 4 & -3 \\ 7 & -5 \end{vmatrix} = (4)(-5) - (-3)(7) = -20 + 21 = 1$$

Substitute these into the expansion to get the determinant:

$$\text{det}(A) = 3(-3\lambda + 15) + 2(4\lambda - 21) + 1(1)$$

$$= 3(-3\lambda + 15) + 2(4\lambda - 21) + 1$$

$$= 3(-3\lambda) + 3(15) + 2(4\lambda) - 2(21) + 1$$

$$= -9\lambda + 45 + 8\lambda - 42 + 1$$

$$= -\lambda + 4$$

For the system to have no solution, $$\text{det}(A)$$ must be zero, because that's when the coefficient matrix does not have full rank and the system is inconsistent:

$$-\lambda + 4 = 0$$

$$\lambda = 4$$

Therefore, the value of $$\lambda$$ that makes the system of linear equations have no solution is $$4$$.

Answer: (A) 4

content: Let $$A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}3 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 5 & 1\end{array}\right]$$ and $$49 B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 13 & -3 \\ -4 & -3 & 12 \\ \alpha & -11 & -5\end{array}\right]$$ If $$B$$ is the inverse of $$A$$, then the value of $$\alpha$$ is
explanation:
Since, $$B$$ is the inverse of $$A$$.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad A \times B=I \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & 5 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 / 49 & 13 / 49 & -3 / 49 \\ -4 / 49 & -3 / 49 & 12 / 49 \\ \alpha / 49 & -11 / 49 & -5 / 49 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} (3-8+3 \alpha / 49 & (39-6-33) / 49 & (-9+24-15) / 49 \\ (4-4+0) / 49 & (52-3+0) / 49 & (-12+12+0) / 49 \\ (2-20+\alpha) / 49 & (26-15-11) / 49 & (-6+60-5) / 49 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{ccc} (-5+3 \alpha) / 49 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ (-18+\alpha) / 49 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{-5+3 \alpha}{49}=1 \text { and } \frac{-18+\alpha}{49}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 \alpha=54 \text { and } \alpha=18 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=18 \text { and } \alpha=18 \end{aligned}$$

Thus, $$\alpha=18$$

content: $$ \text { If } A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sin \theta & -\cos \theta \\ \cos \theta & \sin \theta \end{array}\right] \text {, then } A(\operatorname{adj} A)^{-1} \text { equals to } $$
explanation:
$$\text { Given, } A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sin \theta & -\cos \theta \\ \cos \theta & \sin \theta \end{array}\right]$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \operatorname{adj} A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \sin \theta & -\cos \theta \\ -(-\cos \theta) & \sin \theta \end{array}\right] \\ & {\left[\because \text { If } A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right] \text {, then adj } A=\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -c \\ -b & a \end{array}\right]\right] } \end{aligned} $$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \operatorname{adj} A=A \\ & \begin{aligned} \operatorname{Now,} A\left(\operatorname{adj}(A)^{-1}\right) & =A(A)^{-1} \\ = & I=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \quad\left[\because A A^{-1}=A^{-1} A=I\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$a, b, c$$ are non-zero real numbers and if the system of equations $$(a-1) x-y-z=0, -x+(b-1) y-z=0,-x-y+(c-1) z=0$$ has a non-trivial solution, then $$a b+b c+c a$$ equals to
explanation:
Given, system of equations has a non-trivial solution

$$\therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a-1 & -1 & -1 \\ -1 & b-1 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & c-1 \end{array}\right|=0$$

On applying $$C_2 \rightarrow C_2-C_3$$, we get

$$\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a-1 & 0 & -1 \\ -1 & b & -1 \\ -1 & -c & c-1 \end{array}\right|=0$$

On applying $$R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_3$$, we get

$$\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a-1 & -1 & -1 \\ 0 & b+c & -c \\ -1 & -c & c-1 \end{array}\right|=0$$

On applying $$R_3 \rightarrow R_3-R_1$$, we get

$$\left|\begin{array}{ccc} a-1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & b+c & -c \\ a & -c & c \end{array}\right|=0$$

On expanding along $$R_1$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & (a-1)\left(b c+c^2-c^2\right)-1(a b+a c)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(a-1)(b c)-a b-a c=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a b c-b c-a b-a c=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a b+b c+a c=a b c \\ & \end{aligned}$$

content: Given 2x $$-$$ y + 2z = 2, x $$-$$ 2y - z = $$-$$4, x + y + $$\lambda$$z = 4, then the value of $$\lambda$$ such that the given system of equation has no solution is

Let $$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & { - 1} & 1 \cr 2 & 1 & { - 3} \cr 1 & 1 & 1 \cr } } \right]$$ and $$10B = \left[ {\matrix{ 4 & 2 & 2 \cr { - 5} & 0 & \alpha \cr 1 & { - 2} & 3 \cr } } \right]$$

If B is the inverse of A, then the value of $$\alpha$$ is

explanation:
Given,

$$\left[ {\matrix{ 1 & { - \tan \theta } \cr {\tan \theta } & 1 \cr } } \right]{\left[ {\matrix{ 1 & {\tan \theta } \cr { - \tan \theta } & 1 \cr } } \right]^{ - 1}} = \left[ {\matrix{ a & { - b} \cr b & a \cr } } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left[ {\matrix{ 1 & { - \tan \theta } \cr {\tan \theta } & 1 \cr } } \right]\,.\,{1 \over {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}\left[ {\matrix{ 1 & { - \tan \theta } \cr {\tan \theta } & 1 \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ a & { - b} \cr b & a \cr } } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}\left[ {\matrix{ {1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta } & { - 2\tan \theta } \cr {2\tan \theta } & {1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta } \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ a & { - b} \cr b & a \cr } } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left[ {\matrix{ {{{1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta } \over {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} & {{{2\tan \theta } \over {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \cr {{{2\tan \theta } \over {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} & {{{1 - {{\tan }^2}\theta } \over {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ a & { - b} \cr b & a \cr } } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left[ {\matrix{ {\cos 2\theta } & { - \sin 2\theta } \cr {\sin 2\theta } & {\cos 2\theta } \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ a & { - b} \cr b & a \cr } } \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow a = \cos 2\theta ,b = \sin 2\theta $$

content:
If p $$\ne$$ a, q $$\ne$$ b, r $$\ne$$ c and the system of equations

px + ay + az = 0

bx + qy + bz = 0

cx + cy + rz = 0

has a non-trivial solution, then the value of $$\frac{p}{p-a}+\frac{q}{q-b}+\frac{r}{r-c}$$ is
explanation:
As the given system of equations has a non-trivial solution.

$$\Delta = \left| {\matrix{ p & a & a \cr b & q & b \cr c & c & r \cr } } \right| = 0$$

Applying $${C_2} \to {C_2} - {C_1}$$ and $${C_3} \to {C_3} - {C_1}$$

$$\Delta = \left| {\matrix{ p & {a - p} & {a - p} \cr b & {q - b} & 0 \cr c & 0 & {r - c} \cr } } \right| = 0$$

Expanding along C3, we get

$$(a - p)\left| {\matrix{ b & {q - b} \cr c & 0 \cr } } \right| + (r - c)\left| {\matrix{ p & {a - p} \cr b & {q - b} \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (a - p)( - c)(q - b) + (r - c)\{ p(q - b) - b(a - p)\} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (p - a)(q - b)c + p(r - c)(q - b) + b(r - c)(p - a) = 0$$

Dividing $$(p - a)(q - b)(r - c)$$, we get

$${c \over {r - c}} + {p \over {p - a}} + {b \over {q - b}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {p \over {p - a}} + {q \over {q - b}} + {r \over {r - c}} = {{q - b} \over {q - b}} + {{r - c} \over {r - c}} = 2$$

Given, $$\left| {\matrix{ 0 & {x - p} & {x - q} \cr {x + p} & 0 & {x - r} \cr {x + q} & {x - r} & 0 \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ - (x - p)\left| {\matrix{ {x + p} & {x - r} \cr {x + q} & 0 \cr } } \right| + (x - q)\left| {\matrix{ {x + p} & 0 \cr {x + q} & {x - r} \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - p)(x + q)(x - r) + (x - q)(x + p)(x - r) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - r)[(x - p)(x + q) + (x - q)(x + p)] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - r)[{x^2} - px + qx - pq + {x^2} - qx + px - pq] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - r)[2{x^2} - 2pq] = 0 \Rightarrow x - r = 0$$ or $${x^2} - pq = 0$$

either x = r or pq

content: Matrix $$A = \left| {\matrix{ x & 3 & 2 \cr 1 & y & 4 \cr 2 & 2 & z \cr } } \right|$$, if xyz = 60 and 8x + 4y + 3z = 20, then A(adj A) is equal to
explanation:
A . adj A = | A | I

| A | = xyz $$-$$ 8x $$-$$ 3(z $$-$$ 8) + 2(2 $$-$$ 2y)

| A | = xyz $$-$$ (8x + 3z + 4y) + 28

$$\Rightarrow$$ 60 $$-$$ 20 + 28 = 68

$$\Rightarrow$$ (adj A)$$-$$1 always exists whenever (A)$$-$$1 exists.

$$\therefore$$ A . adj A = | A | I

$$ = 68\left[ {\matrix{ 1 & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & 1 & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & 1 \cr } } \right] = \left[ {\matrix{ {68} & 0 & 0 \cr 0 & {68} & 0 \cr 0 & 0 & {68} \cr } } \right]$$

content: An ordered pair ($$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$) for which the system of linear $$(1 + \alpha )x + \beta y + z = 2$$, $$\alpha x + (1 + \beta )y + z = 3$$, $$\alpha x + \beta y + 2z = 2$$ has a unique solution.
explanation:
We have,

$$(1 + \alpha )x + \beta y + z = 2$$

$$\alpha x + (1 + \beta )y + z = 3$$

$$\alpha x + \beta y + 2z = 2$$

For unique solution $$\left| {\matrix{ {1 + \alpha } & \beta & 1 \cr \alpha & {1 + \beta } & 1 \cr \alpha & \beta & 2 \cr } } \right| \ne 0$$

Apply R1 $$\to$$ R1 - R2 and R2 $$\to$$ R2 - R3, we get

$$\left| {\matrix{ 1 & { - 1} & 0 \cr 0 & 1 & { - 1} \cr \alpha & \beta & 2 \cr } } \right| \ne 0$$

Expanding along R1, we get

$$1(2 + \beta ) + 1(0 + \alpha ) \ne 0$$

$$ = \alpha + \beta + 2 \ne 0$$

Only option (c) satisfied above equation.

content: Consider matrix $$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 2 & 1 \cr 1 & 2 \cr } } \right]$$, if $${A^{ - 1}} = \alpha I + \beta A$$, where $$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$ $$ \notin $$ R, then ($$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$) is equal to (where A$$-$$1 denotes the inverse of matrix A)
explanation:
We have,

$$A = \left[ {\matrix{ 2 & 1 \cr 1 & 2 \cr } } \right]$$

We know that, $$\left| {A \to \lambda I} \right| = 0$$

$$\left| {\left[ {\matrix{ 2 & 1 \cr 1 & 2 \cr } } \right] - \left[ {\matrix{ \lambda & 0 \cr 0 & \lambda \cr } } \right]} \right| = 0$$

$$ = \left| {\matrix{ {2 - \lambda } & 1 \cr 1 & {2 - \lambda } \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(2 - \lambda )^2} - 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4 - 4\lambda + {\lambda ^2} - 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\lambda ^2} - 4\lambda + 3 = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $${A^2} - 4A + 3I = 0$$

Multiply by A$$-$$1, we get

$$A - 4I + 3{A^{ - 1}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {A^{ - 1}} = {{ - 1} \over 3}A + {4 \over 3}I$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha = - {1 \over 3}$$ and $$\beta = {4 \over 3}$$

$$\alpha + \beta = - {1 \over 3} + {4 \over 3} = {3 \over 3} = 1$$
